cases.title: Thyroid FNA: papillary thyroid carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 42-year-old woman with a 1.6 cm hypoechoic thyroid nodule with punctate echogenic foci.

image.caption: Papillary thyroid carcinoma cytology with nuclear grooves and intranuclear pseudoinclusions.

diagnosticSteps: Assess specimen adequacy and identify a cellular follicular-derived epithelial lesion.
Recognize papillary thyroid carcinoma nuclear features including enlargement, powdery chromatin, grooves, pseudoinclusions, and crowding.
Classify using the Bethesda System category and recommend clinical correlation for surgical management.

ancillaryOptions.name: BRAF V600E testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Positive result supports papillary thyroid carcinoma but is not required when cytology is diagnostic.

ancillaryOptions.name: Thyroglobulin needle washout
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful for suspected metastatic thyroid carcinoma in a lymph node, not routine for primary thyroid nodule FNA.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Classic cytomorphology is sufficient for a malignant Bethesda VI interpretation.

cases.reportingTarget: Thyroid, right lobe nodule, fine needle aspiration: Malignant. Papillary thyroid carcinoma, Bethesda category VI.
pitfalls: Do not overcall air-drying artifact or cyst-lining cells as papillary carcinoma.
Do not omit Bethesda category and adequacy statement.

cases.title: Lung FNA: adenocarcinoma
cases.specimenType: CT-guided lung fine needle aspiration with cell block
cases.clinicalHistory: 66-year-old former smoker with a 2.8 cm peripheral right upper lobe mass.

image.caption: Lung adenocarcinoma in cytology cell block with gland-forming malignant epithelial cells.

diagnosticSteps: Confirm a malignant epithelial population in direct smears and/or cell block material.
Evaluate glandular features such as acinar clusters, mucin, cytoplasmic vacuoles, and eccentric nuclei.
Conserve cell block material while ordering a focused immunopanel and molecular studies needed for lung adenocarcinoma management.

ancillaryOptions.name: TTF-1 and Napsin A
ancillaryOptions.result: Nuclear TTF-1 and granular Napsin A positivity support primary lung adenocarcinoma.

ancillaryOptions.name: p40
ancillaryOptions.result: Negative or focal staining helps exclude squamous cell carcinoma when morphology is ambiguous.

ancillaryOptions.name: Broad metastatic carcinoma panel
ancillaryOptions.result: Avoid broad upfront panels if tissue is limited and clinical context supports lung primary.

cases.reportingTarget: Lung, right upper lobe mass, fine needle aspiration: Positive for malignant cells, adenocarcinoma. Immunophenotype supports pulmonary primary. Cell block is available for ancillary testing.
pitfalls: Do not exhaust scant cell block material before required predictive testing.
Do not label as primary lung adenocarcinoma without considering metastatic disease when history or immunophenotype is discordant.

cases.title: Pleural effusion: metastatic breast carcinoma
cases.specimenType: Pleural fluid cytology with cell block
cases.clinicalHistory: 58-year-old woman with history of ER-positive invasive breast carcinoma and new unilateral pleural effusion.

image.caption: Malignant epithelial cells in pleural fluid cell block.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: distribution of patterns of cell arrangement in various malignancies in cell blocks a metastatic adenocarcinoma from the lung b metastatic adenocarcinoma from the breast c metastatic adenocarcinoma from the ovary d metastatic adenocarcinoma from the esophagus e metastatic adenocarcinoma from the colon f metastatic adenocarcinoma from the urothelium g metastatic adenocarcinoma from other sites h malignant mesothelioma i squamous cell carcinoma j lymphoma k myeloma l melanoma m small cell carcinoma n embryonal rhabdomyosarcoma o thymoma and p unclassified tumors numerals inside the figure indicate the number of cases

diagnosticSteps: Distinguish malignant epithelial clusters from reactive mesothelial cells using cytologic features and cell block architecture.
Correlate with prior breast carcinoma history and assess whether the cytology supports metastatic adenocarcinoma.
Use a focused immunopanel to confirm epithelial lineage and support breast origin while excluding mesothelial proliferation.

ancillaryOptions.name: Ber-EP4 or MOC31
ancillaryOptions.result: Membranous positivity supports carcinoma over mesothelial cells.

ancillaryOptions.name: GATA3 and ER
ancillaryOptions.result: Positivity supports metastatic breast carcinoma in the appropriate clinical context.

ancillaryOptions.name: Calretinin and WT1
ancillaryOptions.result: Mesothelial marker staining helps exclude reactive or malignant mesothelial proliferation.

cases.reportingTarget: Pleural fluid, cytology: Positive for malignant cells, metastatic carcinoma consistent with breast primary.
pitfalls: Do not mistake florid reactive mesothelial cells for carcinoma without supportive features.
Do not overstate the primary site if immunostains are limited or discordant.

cases.title: Urine cytology: high-grade urothelial carcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 71-year-old man with painless hematuria and cystoscopic erythematous bladder mucosa.

image.caption: High-grade urothelial carcinoma cells in urine cytology.

diagnosticSteps: Assess adequacy and preservation, including whether degeneration limits interpretation.
Identify high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, hyperchromasia, irregular nuclear membranes, and coarse chromatin in urothelial cells.
Apply the Paris System category and correlate with cystoscopic findings for targeted biopsy.

ancillaryOptions.name: UroVysion FISH
ancillaryOptions.result: Can support urothelial carcinoma detection in selected equivocal or surveillance settings.

ancillaryOptions.name: CK20 and p53 on cell block
ancillaryOptions.result: May support high-grade urothelial carcinoma if sufficient cell block material exists.

ancillaryOptions.name: No ancillary study
ancillaryOptions.result: Classic high-grade malignant cytology is sufficient for a positive Paris System interpretation.

cases.reportingTarget: Urine, voided, cytology: Positive for high-grade urothelial carcinoma, Paris System category VI.
pitfalls: Do not use urine cytology to exclude low-grade papillary urothelial neoplasm.
Do not overcall instrumented or stone-related reactive atypia as high-grade carcinoma.

cases.title: Cervical Pap test: high-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion
cases.clinicalHistory: 31-year-old woman with high-risk HPV positivity on screening.

image.caption: High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion in cervical cytology.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: high grade squamous intra epithelial lesion hsil cells showing high nuclear to cytoplasmic ratio hyperchromatic nuclei liquid based thinprep papanicolaou x100mag

diagnosticSteps: Evaluate specimen adequacy, transformation zone component, and obscuring factors.
Identify small atypical squamous cells with high nuclear-to-cytoplasmic ratio, hyperchromasia, and irregular nuclear contours.
Classify under the Bethesda System and recommend correlation with HPV status and colposcopic biopsy.

ancillaryOptions.name: High-risk HPV testing or genotyping
ancillaryOptions.result: High-risk HPV positivity supports risk stratification and management.

ancillaryOptions.name: p16/Ki-67 dual stain
ancillaryOptions.result: May support triage in selected screening algorithms but is not required when HSIL cytology is diagnostic.

ancillaryOptions.name: Gonorrhea and chlamydia testing
ancillaryOptions.result: Addresses infectious screening only; it does not classify epithelial abnormality.

cases.reportingTarget: Cervical cytology, liquid-based Pap test: High-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (HSIL). Specimen satisfactory for evaluation; transformation zone component present.
pitfalls: Do not downgrade HSIL to LSIL because the abnormal cells are few.
Do not omit adequacy or transformation zone status.

cases.title: Bile duct brushing: adenocarcinoma
cases.clinicalHistory: 67-year-old man with painless jaundice and a distal common bile duct stricture.

image.caption: Malignant glandular cells in bile duct brushing cytology.

sourceArticle.figureCaption: representative brush cytology specimens from patients with severe jaundice left an adequate brush cytology specimen indicating adenocarcinoma cells with atypical nuclei of different sizes disturbance in polarity and clear nucleoli are easily observed right an inadequate specimen obtained from a patient with malignant stricture and severe jaundice cells with large nuclei are seen as an aggregate but the nuclei of the peripheral cells appeared condensed due to damage although the findings suggest malignancy the diagnosis is unclear because the nuclei could not be accurately assessed

diagnosticSteps: Assess cellularity and preservation in the context of brushing artifact and biliary stent-related changes.
Identify malignant glandular features including three-dimensional crowded clusters, nuclear irregularity, prominent nucleoli, and necrotic background.
Correlate with imaging and endoscopic findings and communicate if material is sufficient for cell block or molecular testing.

ancillaryOptions.name: Biliary FISH polysomy panel
ancillaryOptions.result: Can increase support for malignancy in suspicious or atypical biliary brushings.

ancillaryOptions.name: Mucin stain
ancillaryOptions.result: May support glandular differentiation but is rarely decisive in a clearly malignant brushing.

ancillaryOptions.name: Cell block immunohistochemistry
ancillaryOptions.result: Useful if sufficient material is available and metastatic disease is in the differential.

cases.reportingTarget: Common bile duct stricture, brushing cytology: Positive for malignant cells, adenocarcinoma.
pitfalls: Do not overcall reactive atypia from stent, stones, or inflammation without malignant architecture and cytology.
Do not ignore scant cellularity; state limitations when preservation or cellularity is poor.
